source.title: Placebo-induced changes in FMRI in the anticipation and experience of pain.

confidence_rationale: Wager 2004 is the landmark human fMRI study showing that placebo analgesia involves two phases: increased prefrontal activity during pain anticipation, followed by decreased activity in pain-sensitive regions (thalamus, insula, ACC) during pain experience. This two-phase structure maps directly onto the SD-021 mechanism: E3 pre-commit planning (anticipation phase) engages the descending inhibitory pathway, which then reduces z_harm_s PE precision during actual pain exposure. The prefrontal cortex during anticipation is the REE correlate of E3 commitment output activating pgACC -> PAG. The reduced ACC/insula activity during pain experience represents the downstream effect -- suppressed processing of nociceptive input. Two experiments reported using both heat pain and electric shock, with consistent findings. Transfer risk from expectation-based placebo to commitment-gated analgesia remains moderate.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Placebo analgesia is associated with increased prefrontal activity during pain anticipation and decreased activity in pain-sensitive regions (thalamus, insula, ACC) during pain experience.
mapping.ree_translation: The anticipation-phase prefrontal activation is the fMRI correlate of E3 pre-commit trajectory resolution engaging the pgACC -> PAG -> RVM pathway. The pain-experience-phase reduction in thalamus, insula, and ACC activity is the correlate of reduced z_harm_s PE precision -- less nociceptive signal reaching cortical integration stages. The temporal sequence (prefrontal up during anticipation, pain regions down during experience) matches the mechanistic sequence in SD-021 (commitment -> descending inhibition -> reduced sensory-discriminative PE).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Placebo expectation is not equivalent to E3 commitment; placebo acts on anticipated outcome, E3 commitment involves resolved trajectory selection with resource allocation. The ACC reduction here may reflect both sensory-discriminative and affective-motivational suppression conflated -- the study does not separately manipulate intensity vs unpleasantness as Hofbauer 2001 and Rainville 1997 do. Reduced ACC activity in a placebo context may partly reflect suppressed affective processing, which would be at odds with the SD-021 requirement that z_harm_a persists.
mapping.source_context: Two fMRI experiments; Experiment 1: electric shock, n=24; Experiment 2: painful heat, n=23. Placebo cream applied to forearm. Between-subjects comparison of high vs low placebo responders also reported.

confidence_components.notes: Landmark paper with two independent experiments and large samples; mapping caveat about ACC reduction potentially conflating sensory and affective suppression is non-trivial for SD-021's selective attenuation claim; high source quality partially offsets mapping uncertainty
